State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 907 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

August 22, 1977

Laurence M. Watson
Deputy County Counsel
Orange County
P.O. Box 1379
Santa Ana, CA 92702

Re: File No. P-297

Dear Mr. Watson:

Thank you for your letter of August 9, 1977, requesting advice with respect to the filing obligations of Supervisor Anthony. Please find enclosed a copy of a letter from our staff to Terry Nurs regarding similar questions asked by Supervisor Diedrich.

The conclusion that the legal defense fund is political, not personal, with respect to Supervisor Diedrich is equally applicable to Supervisor Anthony. Moreover, the conclusion is even stronger with respect to Supervisor Anthony since a legal defense fund will be administered by his controlled committee and he acknowledges that any surplus funds may be used for future campaign purposes. Accordingly, the supervisor's controlled committee should report all donations to the legal defense fund as contributions.

If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely,

Ted Prim
Staff Counsel
Legal Division
TP:JO

Enclosure

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

August 19, 1977


Terry C. Andrus
Deputy County Counsel
Orange County
P.O. Box 1379
Santa Ana, CA 92702

Re: Opinion Request No. 77-020

Dear Mr. Andrus:

Thank you for your letter of July 27, 1977. You request an opinion concerning the reporting obligations that arise from the establishment of a legal defense fund on behalf of Ralph Diedrich, Orange County Supervisor. Mr. Diedrich was indicted on July 1, 1977, for alleged violations of the reporting requirements of the Political Reform Act. You ask whether monies donated to help defer Mr. Diedrich's legal expenses are reportable as "income" under Chapter 7 of the Act or as "campaign contributions" under Chapter 4. Because your question does not raise a substantial question under the Act, no opinion will be issued. However, I hope you find the following comments helpful.

The Commission has adopted a regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215, which defines a campaign contribution to include:

. . . any monetary or non-monetary payment for which full and adequate consideration is not made to the donor that is received or made at the behest of:

(1) A candidate, unless it is clear from surrounding circumstances that the payment was received or made at his behest for personal purposes unrelated to his candidacy or status as an officeholder. . . .

2 Cal. Adm. Code §18215(b)

Terry C. Andrus
Page two
August 19, 1977


Since the indictment of Mr. Diedrich relates to his
obligations as an officeholder, the standard set forth in the
regulation is met so long as Mr. Diedrich is considered a
"candidate." Government Code Section 82007 defines "candidate"
to mean:

     . . . an individual . . . who receives a contri-
     bution or makes an expenditure or gives his con-
     sent for any other person to receive a contribution or make an expenditure with a view to bring-
     ing about his nomination or election to any elec-
     tive office, whether or not the specific elective
     office for which he will seek nomination or election is known at the time the contribution is re-
     ceived or the expenditure is made and whether or
     not he has announced his candidacy or filed a
     declaration of candidacy at such time.

     You indicate in your letter than "[o]n different
occasions, the Supervisor has publicly expressed an intent not
to run for reelection, as well as an intent to run under certain conditions. His present term expires in 1980." Since the
possibility exists that Mr. Diedrich will run again for elective office, and since, although he is apparently unsure, he
has not categorically ruled out that possibility, we would regard him as a "candidate" within the meaning of Section 82007.
The fact that the outcome of his trial could influence voter
opinion on this question strengthens our view that money collected for this purpose will be more political than personal
in nature.

     This interpretation of Section 82007 is consistent
with previous opinions of the Commission which have held that
left-over campaign funds which are used by an officeholder to
support political activities, such as traveling to speeches
and meetings and printing newsletters, retain their political
character and should be reported as Chapter 4 transactions
even though the election for which the funds were raised has
been concluded. See opinions requested by Janet Gray Hayes,
1 FPPC Ops. 210 (No. 75-145, Dec. 4, 1975) and Willie L.
Brown, Jr., 1 FPPC Ops. 67 (No. 75-055, July 2, 1975).

     The raising of money for a legal defense fund because of possible violations of the Political Reform Act by
a current officeholder seems similarly political in nature.
Accordingly, the appropriate place to disclose these transactions would be on a campaign statement filed pursuant to
Chapter 4.

Terry C. Andrus
Page three
August 19, 1977


If you wish to appeal the denial of your opinion
request, you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18321. If you have any questions, please contact Michael J.
Baker, Chief of the Commission's Legal Division.

Sincerely,

Michael Bennett
Executive Director

ADRIAN KUYPER
County Counsel

WILLIAM J. McCOURT
Chief Assistant

OFFICES OF

ROBERT F. NUTTMAN
ARTHUR C. WAHLSTEDT, JR.
Assistants

THE COUNTY COUNSEL
County Of Orange

JOHN M. PATTERSON
JOHN W. ANDERSON
LAURENCE M. WATSON
VICTOR T. BELLERUE
JACK W. KIRK
CHARLES B. SEVIER
WALTER D. WEBSTER
IRYNE C. BLACK
RALPH W. BENSON
KENNARD R. SMART, JR.
JAMES R. FLOURNOY
TERRY C. ANDRUS
TERRY E. DIXON
RICHARD D. BECK
BARBARA TAM THOMPSON
RICHARD D. OVIEDO
O. M. MOORE
JULEE R. SAMS
NATIVIDAD F. CHAVIRA
BENJAMIN P. de MAYO
RONALD W. STENLACKE
R. DONALD McINTYRE
HOWARD SERBIN
Deputies

COUNTY ADMINISTRATION BUILDING • P.O. BOX 1379 • SANTA ANA, CA 92702 • 834-3300

August 9, 1977

File No. P-297

Fair Political Practices Commission
State of California
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Re: Request for Opinion

Gentlemen:

On behalf of Philip L. Anthony, Orange County Supervisor, and
pursuant to California Government Code Section 83114 and 2 California
Administrative Code Section 18320, your opinion and advice is re-
quested concerning Supervisor Anthony's duties under the Political
Reform Act of 1974 under the following facts:

Supervisor Anthony and others were indicted by the Orange
County Grand Jury on July 1, 1977, for alleged violations
of the Political Reform Act. A number of individuals have
expressed interest in creating a legal defense fund wherein
the monies raised would be used for the Supervisor's legal
defense, and surplus funds, if any, would be either returned
to the donors on a pro rata basis or used by the Supervisor
to help finance his future campaigns. It is contemplated
that contributions for such fund will be received by an
existing committee which has filed a Form 410, "Committee
Statement of Organization," with the Secretary of State.

The question presented for your opinion and advice is whether
monies received for the above-stated purpose are to be considered
"income" reportable under Chapter 7 of the Act, or "campaign contri-
butions" reportable under Chapter 4.

Your prompt attention to this request will be greatly appre-
ciated.

Very truly yours,

ADRIAN KUYPER, COUNTY COUNSEL

By [illegible] M. Paten
Laurence M. Watson, Deputy

LMW:mr/sac
cc: Supervisor Philip L. Anthony

sections.question_synthetic: Whether legal defense fund contributions for indicted county supervisors should be reported as personal income under Chapter 7 or as campaign contributions under Chapter 4 of the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that legal defense fund contributions should be reported as campaign contributions under Chapter 4 because the supervisors remain candidates and the funds are political in nature given the connection to violations of the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, ocr; LLM: Document contains multiple letters from August 1977. Primary FPPC response is the August 19 opinion letter addressing Diedrich case (Opinion Request 77-020). Secondary follow-up letter of August 25 addresses similar Anthony case but references the Diedrich opinion. OCR quality varies; some text distortion present but main sections are legible. No formal CONCLUSION section heading in either letter, but advisory opinions clearly stated.

CONCLUSION: The appropriate place to disclose these transactions would be on a campaign statement filed pursuant to Chapter 4. The Commission regards the supervisors as "candidates" within the meaning of Government Code Section 82007, and the raising of money for a legal defense fund because of possible violations of the Political Reform Act by a current officeholder is political in nature, not personal.

embedding.summary: The FPPC provides advice on two related requests regarding reporting obligations for legal defense funds established on behalf of Orange County Supervisors Ralph Diedrich and Philip L. Anthony, who were indicted for alleged violations of the Political Reform Act. The Commission concludes that contributions to these defense funds should be reported as campaign contributions under Chapter 4, not as personal income.
